---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
##library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)

library(gganimate)
```

class: slideblue

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size45[
Approaches to Environmental Policy Design

+ "Green" Subsidy Policies
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Prep email with link to Google sheet for collecting case evidence
    - https://docs.google.com/document/d/1NU_azo7WnABnc2Hz_w_fqu4mfLGEBC-P-ZuhQSIr0yo/edit?usp=sharing



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

# Paper 2
.size45[
Which of the four policy design options is the "right" choice for addressing your environmental problem?

- Analyze the pros and cons of each option for your chosen problem, and
- Support all claims with evidence
]

???

Keep your eye on the next target: Paper 2.

In that paper you will be asked to evaluate all four of the approaches we study in class from the perspective of your chosen environmental problem.

### Any questions on the prompt?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle, center

# Policy Types
.size55[
.textred[**Command and Control**]

"Green" Taxes

"Green" Subsidies

Adaptive Governance
]

???

Two weeks ago we analyzed the use of C&C regulations to address environmental problems.

### Sum it up for me, what does it mean to use C&C regulations to address an environmental problem?

(SLIDE)



---

class: middle

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.asp=0.618, fig.align='center', out.width='95%', fig.width=6, cache=TRUE}
## Let's build supply and demand curves that move with regulations increasing MC
## Have to install transformr for lines moving
library(gganimate)

d1 <- tibble(
  Quantity = 1:100,
  Supply1 = 12 + .5*Quantity,
  Supply2 = 42 + .5*Quantity,
  Demand = 100 - 1*Quantity
) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = Supply1:Supply2, names_to = "Scenario", values_to = "Value")

d1 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Quantity)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Demand), color = "red", size = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Value), color = "blue", size = 1.2) +
  theme_classic() +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 125)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 20)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 20), limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(x = "Quantity Produced", y = "Price per Widget",
       title = "C&C Regulations: Increasing the MCs") +
  annotate("text", x = 115, y = 5, label = "Demand", size = 5, color = "red", hjust = .5) +
  annotate("text", x = 119, y = 80, label = "Marginal\nCost", size = 5, color = "blue", hjust = .5) +
  transition_states(Scenario, wrap=FALSE) +
  shadow_wake(wake_length = .5, colour = "grey")
```

???

### Refresh my memory, why do C&C regs raise the MC line?
- (Require technology to adopt, OR)
- (Enforce firm limit on emissions)

<br>

### What are the pros and cons of choosing this policy design approach?
- *ON BOARD*: Pros vs Cons

- Pros: Can tackle big, hard to address problems (massive costs, super long time horizons), most effective when piggybacking on established institutions for infrastructure/oversight/enforcement
- Cons: Vulnerable to lobbying, Government moves slowly, Very often imposes actual costs on real people in the short term (need for cushioning?), Must be able to "keep up with" changes in the problem or economy over time (drift problem)

<br>

### What examples from our case studies were the most persuasive endorsements of this approach? Why?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle, center

# Policy Types
.size55[
Command and Control

.textred[**"Green" Taxes**]

"Green" Subsidies

Adaptive Governance
]

???

Last week we analyzed the use of "green" taxes to address environmental problems.

### Sum it up for me, what does it mean to use a "green" tax or pollution charge to address an environmental problem?


---

class: middle

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.asp=0.618, fig.align='center', out.width='98%', fig.width=6, cache=TRUE}
tibble(
  Abatement_cost = 0:100,
  Pollution = 0:100
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Pollution, y = Abatement_cost)) +
  geom_point(color = "white") +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 25), labels = str_c("$", seq(0, 100, 25))) +
  labs(x = "Quantity of Pollution", y = "Price per unit of Pollution") +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 100, y = 100, yend = 0, color = "blue", size = 1.3) +
  annotate("text", x = 87, y = 30, label = "Marginal cost\nof abatement") +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 100, y = 0, yend = 100, color = "red", size = 1.3) +
  annotate("text", x = 82, y = 95, label = "Marginal external\n cost") +
  annotate("segment", x = 50, xend = 50, y = 0, yend = 50, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 50, y = 50, yend = 50, linetype = "dashed") +
  ggtitle("The Basics of Taxing Pollution: Pigou's Solution")
```

???

### Refresh my memory, how does this visualization help us think about "green" taxes?
- So, the aim, according to Pigou, is to choose a tax rate that balances the harms done by pollution against the harms done to the economy
- Set a rate at the equilibrium because more taxes do more damage to the economy than provide benefits, and fewer taxes do more harm to society than benefit the economy.

<br>

### What are the pros and cons of choosing this policy design approach?
- *ON BOARD*: Pros vs Cons

- Pros: Promises efficient pollution cuts, incentivizes cleaner processes, ?
- Cons: Also vulnerable to lobbying, Very hard to determine the equilibrium point between abatement and external costs, ?

<br>

(SLIDE: The aim is to achieve pollution cuts in the most efficient manner possible across the entire economy.)

(SLIDE: Might also incentivize innovation!)



---

class: middle, center

.size50[**1. Green taxes promise more efficient pollution control**]

.pull-left[
```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', out.width='100%', fig.asp=0.85, fig.width=5.5}
## Niskanen examples in Fig 2a and 2b
## Green tax approach: $50 tax
p3 <- tibble(
  Abatement_cost = 0:100,
  Pollution = 0:100
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Pollution, y = Abatement_cost)) +
  geom_point(color = "white") +
  theme_classic() +
  #coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 125)) +
  #scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 25), limits = c(0,100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 25), labels = str_c("$", seq(0, 100, 25))) +
  labs(x = "Quantity of Pollution", y = "Price per unit of Pollution") 

p4a <- p3 +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 100, y = 0, yend = 100, color = "red", size = 1.3) +
  annotate("text", x = 82, y = 95, label = "Marginal external\n cost") +
  geom_abline(intercept = 75, slope = -1, color = "blue", size = 1.3) +
  annotate("text", x = 90, y = 7, label = "Marginal cost\nof abatement") +
  ggtitle("Firm A") 

p4a +
  annotate("polygon", x = c(24.5, 75, 24.5), y = c(0, 0, 50), fill = "lightblue", alpha = .5) +
  annotate("point", x = 75, y = 0, size = 5, color = "steelblue3") +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 100, y = 50, yend = 50, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("point", x = 24.5, y = 50, size = 5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 24.5, xend = 24.5, y = 0, yend = 50, linetype = "dashed")
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', out.width='100%', fig.asp=0.85, fig.width=5.5}
p4b <- p3 +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 100, y = 0, yend = 100, color = "red", size = 1.3) +
  annotate("text", x = 120, y = 95, label = "Marginal\n external\n cost") +
  geom_abline(intercept = 125, slope = -1, color = "blue", size = 1.3) +
  annotate("text", x = 115, y = 37, label = "Marginal cost\nof abatement") +
  ggtitle("Firm B") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 125))

p4b +
  annotate("polygon", x = c(75.5, 125, 75.5), y = c(0, 0, 50), fill = "lightblue", alpha = .5) +
  annotate("point", x = 75.5, y = 50, size = 5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 125, y = 50, yend = 50, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", x = 75.5, xend = 75.5, y = 0, yend = 50, linetype = "dashed")
```
]

???

#### Tax Advantage 1

At a $50 tax one firm cuts more than its equilibrium and the other cuts less.

- HOWEVER, on balance this break-down of cuts gets us to the same goal AT A LOWER ECONOMIC COST.

<br>

The green tax also has the capacity to directly incentivize innovation!




---

class: middle, center

.size50[**2. Green taxes incentivize innovation**]

.pull-left[
```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.asp=0.9, fig.align='center', out.width='98%', fig.width=6, cache=TRUE}
## ANIMATED: Falling Abatement Costs
d2 <- tibble(
  Quantity = 1:100,
  Abatement_cost1 = 100 + -1*Quantity,
  Abatement_cost2 = 80 + -1*Quantity,
  External_cost = 0 + 1*Quantity
) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = Abatement_cost1:Abatement_cost2, names_to = "Scenario", values_to = "Value") |>
  mutate(
    Emitted = if_else(Scenario == "Abatement_cost1", 50, 29),
    Taxed = 50
  )

d2 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Quantity)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = External_cost), color = "red", size = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Value), color = "blue", size = 1.2) +
  theme_classic() +
  #coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 125)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 25), labels = str_c("$", seq(0, 100, 25)), limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(x = "Quantity of Pollution", y = "Price per unit of Pollution",
       title = "Lowering your abatements costs...") +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 100, y = 50, yend = 50, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = 87, y = 30, label = "Marginal cost\nof abatement") +
  annotate("text", x = 82, y = 95, label = "Marginal external\n cost") +
  transition_states(Scenario, wrap=FALSE) +
  #shadow_wake(wake_length = .25, colour = "grey") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = Emitted, xend = Emitted, y = 0, yend = Taxed), linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(aes(x = Emitted, y = Taxed), size = 5) +
  geom_point(aes(x = Emitted, y = Emitted), size = 5)
  # annotate("segment", x = 50, xend = 50, y = 0, yend = 50, linetype = "dashed") +
  # annotate("segment", x = 29, xend = 29, y = 0, yend = 50, linetype = "dashed")
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.asp=0.9, fig.align='center', out.width='98%', fig.width=6, cache=TRUE}
## ANIMATED: Falling MC
d1 <- tibble(
  Quantity = 1:100,
  Supply1 = 42 + .5*Quantity,
  Supply2 = 22 + .5*Quantity,
  Demand = 100 - 1*Quantity
) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = Supply1:Supply2, names_to = "Scenario", values_to = "Value")

d1 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Quantity)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Demand), color = "red", size = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Value), color = "blue", size = 1.2) +
  theme_classic() +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 125)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 20)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 20), limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(x = "Quantity Produced", y = "Price per Widget",
       title = "...lowers your marginal costs overall") +
  annotate("text", x = 115, y = 5, label = "Demand", size = 5, color = "red", hjust = .5) +
  annotate("text", x = 119, y = 85, label = "Supply\n(MC)", size = 5, color = "blue", hjust = .5) +
  transition_states(Scenario, wrap=FALSE) #+
  #shadow_wake(wake_length = .5, colour = "grey")
```
]

???

#### Tax Advantage 2

By taxing the externality we create a market incentive for all firms to cut their pollution.

Your path to greater profits can come from making cleaner products.

- As you cut your emissions you can push your production higher to capture more of the supply at a lower cost!

<br>

### What examples from our case studies were the most persuasive endorsements of this approach? Why?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/12-1-Green_Subsidies_v2.png')
background-size: 80%
background-position: bottom
class: top, center

.size60[**Policy Design Option 3: "Green" Subsidies**]

???

This week we shift to your next option: "Green" subsidies

### Per the Tarver (2021) article on Investopedia, and before we get into the critiques, talk to me about how this approach tries to solve an environmental problem.

- Government provides the money you need to make "better" (e.g. cleaner.more environmental) choices! 

<br>

There are two broad ways to do this even though both basically have the same effect

SLIDE: Pay subsidies directly to industry



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/12-1-manufacturing_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: bottom, center, inverse

.size60[**Paying Subsidies to Industry**]

???

### Tell me this story using the plots we've been using to think about the effects of policy on the economics of production.    

### - How do subsidies to industry help us solve an environmental problem?

(SLIDE)



---

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.asp=0.618, fig.align='center', out.width='98%', fig.width=6, cache=TRUE}
## ANIMATED: Falling Abatement Costs
d3 <- tibble(
  Quantity = 1:100,
  Abatement_cost1 = 100 + -1*Quantity,
  Abatement_cost2 = 70 + -1*Quantity,
  External_cost = 0 + 1*Quantity
) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = Abatement_cost1:Abatement_cost2, names_to = "Scenario", values_to = "Value") |>
  mutate(
    Emitted = if_else(Scenario == "Abatement_cost1", 50, 35)
  )

d3 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Quantity)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = External_cost), color = "red", size = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Value), color = "blue", size = 1.2) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 25), labels = str_c("$", seq(0, 100, 25)), limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(x = "Quantity of Pollution", y = "Price per unit of Pollution",
       title = "Green subsidies directly lower your abatement costs") +
  annotate("text", x = 87, y = 30, label = "Marginal cost\nof abatement") +
  annotate("text", x = 82, y = 95, label = "Marginal external\n cost") +
  transition_states(Scenario, wrap=FALSE) +
  #shadow_wake(wake_length = .05, colour = "grey") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = Emitted, y = Emitted, yend = Emitted), linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = Emitted, xend = Emitted, y = 0, yend = Emitted), linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(aes(x = Emitted, y = Emitted), size = 5)
```

???

Gov't essentially pays firms to adopt cleaner/more efficient processes of production

- Not really focused on supply/demand curves at all

<br>

This is a direct payment to a specific industry that enables it to continue producing the widgets we want but more efficiently.

- Typically take the form of tax credits or reimbursements paid to the company "for part of the cost of the production of a good or service"

<br>

### Anybody know why government often uses tax credits rather than direct subsidies in most instances of "green" subsidy?

- (Much more palatable politically)
    - Cutting taxes for business = good
    - Government handouts to business = less good
    - Lowers revenue as opposed to raising spending
    
- (Considerably easier to adjust tax policy (established processes) vs creating new government spending with legislation)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/12-1-consumers_v2.png')
background-size: 90%
background-position: bottom
class: top, center

.size55[**Paying Subsidies to Consumers**]

???

### Tell me this story using the plots we've been using to think about the effects of policy on the economics of production.    

### - How do subsidies to consumers help us solve an environmental problem?

(SLIDE)



---

class: middle, center

.size50[**Green Subsidies: Picking Winners and Losers**]

.pull-left[
```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', out.width='100%', fig.asp=0.85, fig.width=5.5, cache=TRUE}
## Dirty Firm: Animate decreasing demand line
library(gganimate)

d10a <- tibble(
  Quantity = 1:100,
  Demand1 = 100 - 1*Quantity,
  Demand2 = 80 - 1*Quantity,
  Supply = 0 + 1*Quantity
) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = Demand1:Demand2, names_to = "Scenario", values_to = "Value")

d10a |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Quantity)) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 100, y = 100, yend = 0, color = "darkgrey", size = 1.25) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Value), color = "red", size = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Supply), color = "blue", size = 1.2) +
  theme_classic() +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 125)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 20)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 20), limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(x = "Quantity Produced", y = "Price per Widget",
       title = "Consumer Subsidies: Leaving a Dirty Firm Behind") +
  annotate("text", x = 115, y = 5, label = "Demand", size = 5, color = "red", hjust = .5) +
  annotate("text", x = 119, y = 85, label = "Supply\n(MC)", size = 5, color = "blue", hjust = .5) +
  transition_states(Scenario, wrap=FALSE)
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', out.width='100%', fig.asp=0.85, fig.width=5.5, cache=TRUE}
## Cleaner Firm: Animate increasing demand line
d10b <- tibble(
  Quantity = 1:100,
  Demand1 = 100 - 1*Quantity,
  Demand2 = 120 - 1*Quantity,
  Supply = 0 + 1*Quantity
) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = Demand1:Demand2, names_to = "Scenario", values_to = "Value")

d10b |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Quantity)) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 100, y = 100, yend = 0, color = "darkgrey", size = 1.25) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Value), color = "red", size = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Supply), color = "blue", size = 1.2) +
  theme_classic() +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 125)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 20)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 20), limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(x = "Quantity Produced", y = "Price per Widget",
       title = "Consumer Subsidies: Boosting a Clean Firm") +
  annotate("text", x = 115, y = 5, label = "Demand", size = 5, color = "red", hjust = .5) +
  annotate("text", x = 119, y = 85, label = "Supply\n(MC)", size = 5, color = "blue", hjust = .5) +
  transition_states(Scenario, wrap=FALSE)
```
]

???

If demand for a product or service is low, tax credits or rebates can help offset the high cost.

- The subsidy increases the demand line for the "preferred" firm and lowers it for the "dirty" firm.

- Usually through tax credits, e.g. solar panels for your house or buying an electric vehicle

This is absolutely having the government pick winners and losers.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size55[**Targeting Government Subsidies**]]

<br>

.pull-left[
```{r, fig.retina=3, out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/12-1-consumers_v2.png')
```

.center[.size40[Consumers]]
]

.pull-right[
```{r, fig.retina=3, out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/12-1-manufacturing_v2.png')
```

.center[.size40[Industry]]
]

???

Importantly, whichever way we go with this the aim is basically the same.

- Using tax dollars to boost services or industries we like at the expense of those we do not.

- Whether paid to industry or consumers, both paths have the same effects (lower abatement costs, more supply, more demand)

<br>

### Questions on the basics here?

<br>

Let's segue to the environment more directly.


---

class: middle, slideblue

.size60[**Subsidies for Pollution Control**]

.size30[
"Subsidies are forms of **financial government support** for activities believed to be environmentally friendly. Rather than charging a polluter for emissions, a subsidy rewards a polluter for reducing emissions. Examples of subsidies include **grants, low-interest loans, favorable tax treatment, and procurement mandates**. Subsidies have been used for a wide variety of purposes, including: brownfield development after a hazardous substance contamination; agricultural grants for erosion control; low-interest loans for small farmers; grants for land conservation; and loans and grants for recycling industrial, commercial and residential products. **While subsidies offer incentives to reduce emissions similar to a tax, they also encourage market entry to qualify for the subsidy**" ([Link](https://www.epa.gov/environmental-economics/economic-incentives#subsidies)).
]

???

Here is how the EPA defines "Subsidies for Pollution Control."

### Everybody clear on the definition as applied to environmental problems?

#### - Any of these program types you're not familiar with?

<br>

### Everybody clear on what kinds of programs you'll need to find for our case work on Thursday?

<br>

Well, as you know from the readings for today, subsidies make a lot of people mad!

- We need to take these arguments seriously if we intend to use subsidies as our chosen policy tool.

In a sense, my challenge for us today is to see these critiques **NOT** as deal-breakers, but instead as **design challenges**!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/12-1-heritage_loris2011.png')
background-size: 85%
background-position: center

???

Let's start with our old friend from the Heritage Foundation Nicolas Loris.

### Assuming this is a challenge for subsidy design and not a deal-breaker, what do we need to keep in mind when designing a subsidy to address an environmental problem?

*Build a list ON THE BOARD across the articles*

- ?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[**Are Clean Energy Tax Credits Equitable? (Davis 2015)**]]

<br>

.pull-left[
```{r, fig.retina=3, out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/12-1-Borenstein2016-FigA.png')
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, fig.retina=3, out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/12-1-Borenstein2016-FigC.png')
```
]

???

### Assuming this is a challenge for subsidy design and not a deal-breaker, what do we need to keep in mind when designing a subsidy to address an environmental problem?

*Continue building our list ON THE BOARD across the articles*

- ?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/12-1-Best2017.png')
background-size: 85%
background-position: center

???

I grant you, the tone of this article undermines its credibility somewhat, however, still covers some good research in a simple way that should help us in our design exercise.

### Assuming this is a challenge for subsidy design and not a deal-breaker, what do we need to keep in mind when designing a subsidy to address an environmental problem?

*Continue building our list ON THE BOARD across the articles*

- ?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/12-1-renewables_for_all.png')
background-size: 85%
background-position: center

???

Our last critique / piece of design advice comes from the formation of a recent community activist coalition called "Residential Renewables for All."

### What specifically is this group asking policy designers to keep in mind when using subsidies to address environmental problems?

#### - What is "direct pay" and how would it address the equity concerns about green subsidies?

(SLIDE)



---

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='95%', fig.width=6}
## Non-refundable $1,000 Credit
nonref <- tibble(
  Policy = "Non-refundable",
  Taxes = seq(0, 2000, 100),
  Subsidy = 1000
  ) |>
  mutate(
    Net = if_else(Taxes - Subsidy < 0, Taxes, Subsidy)
  )

nonref |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Taxes, y = Net)) +
  geom_line(color = "red", size = 1.2) +
  labs(x = "Taxes Owed ($)", y = "Subsidy Received ($)",
       title = "Hypothetical $1k 'Green' Subsidy'") +
  theme_minimal() +
  annotate("text", x = 1800, y = 900, label = "Non-Refundable", color = "red")
```

???

This plot illustrates the effect of delivering a $1k green subsidy as a non-refundable tax credit.
- The x-axis shows how much a person owes in federal taxes.
- The y-axis is how much of the $1k subsidy they will actually receive.

Most tax credits are non-refundable
- They reduce or eliminate the income tax you owe

So, if you don't owe anything (because you make too little money) the credit = $0 for you!

<br>

On the one hand, it's super attractive for government to use the tax code to deliver subsidies.

+ We already have a massive tax infrastructure with many other tax credits built into it.

On the other hand, this shows why using tax credits is such a problem.

+ For anyone who owes less than the amount of the subsidy, they are only eligible for a partial amount.
    + You can't get back more than you owe.
    
+ For those with no federal tax obligation they get NONE of the subsidy!



---

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='95%', fig.width=6}
## Non-refundable $1,000 Credit
ref <- tibble(
  Policy = "Refundable",
  Taxes = seq(0, 2000, 100),
  Subsidy = 1000
  ) |>
  mutate(
    Net = 1000
  )

rbind(nonref, ref) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Taxes, y = Net, color = Policy, linetype = Policy)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  labs(x = "Taxes Owed ($)", y = "Subsidy Received ($)",
       title = "Hypothetical $1k 'Green' Subsidy'") +
  theme_minimal() +
  annotate("text", x = 1800, y = 900, label = "Non-Refundable", color = "red") +
    annotate("text", x = 200, y = 900, label = "Refundable", color = "blue") +
  guides(color = "none", linetype = "none") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("red", "blue"))
```

???

The 300 environmental justice advocates, environmental groups, and renewable energy companies in Residential Renewables for All are calling for a refundable credit.

This kind of credit is refundable, meaning that if you owe less in taxes than the size of the subsidy the government sends you a check for the difference.

This approach guarantees everyone has access to the full subsidy.

### Anybody want to guess why the great majority of tax credits are non-refundable rather than refundable?

<br>

However, 

1. If the goal is to get more people to pay for more efficient products and services, and

2. We are worried about equity in our society, 

Then we need to think about this carefully.

<br>

As an important aside, how big is this problem?

### Anybody know what proportion of federal tax filers owe nothing?

(SLIDE)

##### Notes

#### Regan, L., Wong, B., Preston, B. L., & Curtright, A. E. (2021). Incentivizing Solar: Catalyzing Solar Energy Technology Adoption to Address the Challenge of Climate Change. RAND Corporation. https://www.rand.org/pubs/perspectives/PEA1372-1.html

+ "In contrast to nonrefundable credits, refundable tax credits can be monetized without any tax liability. They have been referred to as direct pay tax credits because the Department of the Treasury effectively pays eligible participants the difference if the value of the credit exceeds the tax liability, even if the liability is zero" (9).

+ "These direct payments were treated as an overpayment of taxes and were able to be monetized as cash refunds after filing a federal tax return" (10).

+ "If the original intention of the ITC was to decrease solar installation costs, spur industry growth, and reduce barriers to market entry, reenabling the refundable provisions would eliminate dependence on tax equity financing and tax credit rollover. Direct pay is a more efficient project finance option, eliminating the premium paid to tax equity and third-party investors for project financing and ensuring that project installers receive the full benefit. There are other benefits that the refundable tax credit provides besides effective financial utilization and equity. Between 2009 and 2011, the refundable solar tax credit program allowed 110,000 projects to monetize the credit. The largest number of projects were residential solar systems. Additionally, the program was attributed with the creation of 75,000 direct and indirect jobs (Steinberg, Porro, and Goldberg, 2012). However, the refundable solar tax credit proved expensive because of the high uptake. Therefore, expanding access to the ITC for more individuals ultimately results in the federal government bearing a greater burden of solar generation deployment costs" (18-19).



---

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='95%', fig.width=6}
library(readxl)
d <- read_excel("libs/Data/TaxPolicyCenter_No_Tax_2021.xlsx") |>
  mutate(
    Row = 1:11, 
    pct_nonpaying = pct_nonpaying/100,
    pct_joint = pct_joint/100,
    pct_wchildren = pct_wchildren/100
  )

d |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Income, Row), y = pct_nonpaying)) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = seq(.25, .75, .25), color = "white") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Income Level", y = "",
       title = "All Tax Units with Zero Income Tax (2021)",
       caption = "Source: Tax Policy Center") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format())
```

???

Data from the nonpartisan Tax Policy Center (jointly operated by the Urban Institute and the Brookings Institution) [Link](https://www.taxpolicycenter.org/model-estimates/tax-units-zero-or-negative-income-tax-liability-august-2021/t21-0162-distribution)

Here we see the proportion of all tax filers in income categories who owe zero federal income taxes (after credits and deductions).

### What do we learn from this?

- (HUGE swathes of American families would receive nothing from a non-refundable subsidy)

- (Pretty clear why the research shows green subsidies are inequitable!)

<br>

If you think that's something, take a look at the breakdown for tax filers with children

SLIDE



---

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='95%', fig.width=6}
d |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(pct_nonpaying, pct_wchildren), names_to = "Category", values_to = "Values") |>
  mutate(
    Category = if_else(Category == "pct_wchildren", "With Children", "All Filers")
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Income, Row), y = Values, fill = Category)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = seq(.25, .75, .25), color = "white") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Income Level", y = "",
       title = "All Tax Units with Zero Income Tax (2021)",
       caption = "Source: Tax Policy Center",
       fill = "") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("red", "blue"))  +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE))
```

???

That's just nuts!

### So, where do we end up on the question of subsidies as a policy tool for addressing environmental problems?

#### - What are the pros and cons of using subsidies to address an environmental problem?

*ON BOARD*

Pros: 

- ?

Cons: 

- ?



---

class: middle, slideblue

.size50[**Thursday: "Green" Subsidy Cases**]

.size45[
Find a real world example of .textblue[**this approach**] being used to .textblue[**successfully**] address an environmental problem .textblue[**similar to the one in your project**].
]

.size45[
.textblue[**Present as an argument**]: This case shows that addressing environmental problem X can be done successfully using "green" subsidies.
]

???

### Questions?
